humanities_role_explanation: Evaluates LLMs on domain-specific research tasks (such as transcription, layout segmentation, and structured metadata extraction) using real, expert-annotated historical and archival source materials from humanities consulting projects.
benchmark_modalities_explanation: Combines scanned document images (historical letters, index cards, print pages, and medieval manuscripts) with text-based instructions, alongside text-only tasks like XML error correction.
analytical_tasks_explanation: Evaluates models on handwritten and printed (Fraktur) text recognition, document layout segmentation, multi-stage metadata extraction/NER from historical lists and library cards, and logical/structural correction of LLM-generated XML files.

general_purpose_benchmark.note: Specifically designed as a small, specialized, domain-specific digital humanities evaluation framework rather than a general-purpose benchmark.

evaluates_generative_llm.note: Evaluates models such as OpenAI's GPT-4, GPT-4o, GPT-4.1-mini, and GPT-5, Anthropic's Claude Sonnet 4.5, and Google's Gemini 2.0 Pro.

llm_is_primary_system.note: The LLMs (and vision-language models) are the primary systems being evaluated on their processing and extraction performance.

arts_humanities_data.note: Uses historical, literary, and archival sources corresponding to ASJC areas: History, Language and Linguistics, Philosophy, Literature and Literary Theory, and Classics.

quantitative_metrics.note: Applies quantitative metrics including fuzzy string matching, F1 scores, and Character Error Rates (CER).

english_language.note: The paper is written in English, and the benchmark includes English tasks (along with multilingual items in German, French, Latin, and Greek).

summary: The paper introduces the RISE Humanities Data Benchmark, a domain-specific evaluation framework designed to assess large language models on complex digital humanities workflows. The benchmark features eight curated, expert-verified datasets representing real-world challenges like transcription of historical scripts and structured metadata extraction. It provides a standardized execution pipeline and custom web interface to track and compare model performance over time.
key_findings: OpenAI's GPT-5 achieved the highest scores on complex extraction tasks such as Business Letters (77.00%), Company Lists (58.40%), and Library Cards (89.51%). Anthropic's Claude Sonnet 4.5 excelled at structured XML correction (97.47%), while Google's Gemini 2.0 Pro achieved 95.70% on Fraktur newspaper page segmentation and transcription.
